text:
The primary objective of this research aim is to identify cytokine candidates that exhibit optimal synergistic effects when combined with the anti-TREM2 monoclonal antibody (mAb), E3C7. The working hypothesis posits that specific combinations of pro-inflammatory cytokines will enhance the therapeutic efficacy of the anti-TREM2 mAb in modulating immune responses, particularly in myeloid, NK, and T cell populations. This aim encompasses a systematic screening of approximately 30 cytokines, including but not limited to CCL3, IL-6, IL-12, and CXCL10, to evaluate their effects on immune cell activity and tumor cell killing assays. The overarching goal is to elucidate the most effective cytokine combinations that can be leveraged to improve anti-TREM2 mAb efficacy in therapeutic contexts.

 The methodology involves five distinct tasks: first, measuring the effects of cytokines and anti-TREM2 mAb on myeloid, NK, and T cell activity through co-culture assays, which will provide insights into T-NK cell activation and cytokine secretion profiles. Second, conducting killing assays with co-cultured human-derived cancer cells to assess the cytotoxic potential of the identified cytokine-mAb combinations. Third, selecting the most effective cytokines based on their performance in the aforementioned assays. Fourth, employing single-cell analysis using an in-vitro functional screening system to dissect the cellular responses to the cytokine-mAb combinations at a granular level. Finally, performing single-cell analysis within an in vivo screening system to validate the findings in a more physiologically relevant context.

 The expected results include the identification of cytokine candidates that significantly enhance the efficacy of the anti-TREM2 mAb, leading to improved immune activation and tumor cell clearance. This research aims to provide a molecular understanding of the synergistic interactions between cytokines and the anti-TREM2 mAb, ultimately contributing to the development of more effective immunotherapeutic strategies for cancer treatment. The successful completion of this aim will lay the groundwork for subsequent objectives focused on the development and validation of lead MiTE molecules, thereby advancing the therapeutic potential of this approach.
title: Discovery campaign of cytokine candidates with optimal synergistic effects with anti-TREM2 antibody
